seo.title: SO1 Annual Return Jamaica 2026: Complete Employer Filing Guide
seo.description: Every Jamaican employer must file the SO1/SO2 annual return with TAJ by March 31, 2026. Complete guide: who must file, what to include, how to avoid penalties, and how to reconcile your year-end payroll data.

title: SO1 Annual Return Jamaica 2026: Complete Employer Filing Guide
subtitle: File your S01/S02 by March 31, 2026 — avoid TAJ penalties with this step-by-step guide

ctaInline.headline: Automate your SO1 preparation
ctaInline.text: Payroll Jamaica generates your SO1/SO2 data automatically — no manual spreadsheets.

ctaBottom.headline: Never miss a March 31 deadline again
ctaBottom.text: Get started with Payroll Jamaica and automate all statutory annual filings.

html:
Every Jamaican employer must file an SO1/SO2 annual return with Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ) by March 31, 2026. The SO1 (Employer Annual Return) summarises total emoluments and all statutory deductions — PAYE, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax — for each employee during the tax year. Miss the deadline and you face a JMD $5,000 fixed penalty plus 50% annual interest on any underpaid PAYE. This guide covers everything you need to file correctly.

Related Resources
• Payroll Software Jamaica — generates SO1/SO2 automatically from your payroll records
• Free Payroll Calculator — verify PAYE, NIS, NHT calculations before filing
• HR Software Jamaica — full employee records for accurate annual returns

What is the SO1 Annual Return?

The SO1 (also called the Employer Annual Return or S01) is a statutory declaration filed with TAJ that reconciles every employee's annual emoluments and deductions against your 12 monthly PAYE remittances. The S02 is the accompanying employer-level aggregate summary. Together, they allow TAJ to identify any underpayment of PAYE, NIS, NHT, or Education Tax for the full tax year (April 1, 2025 to March 31, 2026).

Who Must File?

Every registered employer in Jamaica who paid emoluments to one or more employees during the 2025/2026 tax year. This includes:

  • Companies, sole traders, partnerships, and non-profits
  • Employers of casual, part-time, seasonal, and full-time staff
  • Employers who only had staff for part of the year
  • Employers of domestic workers

There are no minimum earnings or hours thresholds — any employment relationship in the tax year creates an S01 obligation.

The March 31, 2026 Deadline

March 31, 2026 is both the last day of Jamaica's tax year and the hard deadline for S01/S02 submission. In 2026, March 31 falls on a Tuesday — no extension applies. Penalties begin accruing April 1:

  • Fixed penalty: JMD $5,000 per return not filed
  • Interest on underpaid PAYE: 50% per annum, calculated daily
  • Audit risk: Late filers are disproportionately selected for TAJ compliance reviews

What Information Goes on the SO1?

For each employee, you must provide:

  • Full name and TRN (Tax Registration Number) — mandatory; incorrect TRNs result in rejection
  • NIS number
  • Total gross emoluments paid April 1, 2025 – March 31, 2026
  • Total PAYE deducted
  • Total NIS employee contributions deducted
  • Total NHT employee contributions deducted
  • Total Education Tax deducted
  • Non-cash benefits (company car, housing allowance, health insurance)
  • Pension contributions

The S02 aggregates all of the above across your entire workforce. Both the S01 totals and the S02 total must reconcile exactly to your 12 monthly P6 remittance schedules.

Step-by-Step Filing Guide

Step 1 — Gather 12 months of payroll data

Pull records for every employee for every pay period from April 1, 2025 to March 31, 2026. With payroll software, this is a single export. With spreadsheets, you need to compile each employee's monthly figures manually.

Step 2 — Reconcile against monthly remittances

Your annual PAYE total must equal the sum of 12 monthly P6 payments. Identify any gaps or calculation errors and correct them before filing — voluntary corrections attract lower penalties than TAJ-discovered discrepancies.

Step 3 — Complete individual S01 forms

Include all emoluments — salary, overtime, bonuses, service charges, and non-cash benefits. Omitting service charge distributions and casual workers are the two most common errors.

Step 4 — Complete the S02 employer summary

Total all S01 figures. Verify S02 totals match the sum of all individual S01 entries before submission.

Step 5 — Submit to TAJ

File at any TAJ office or via the myTax online portal. Electronic submission is recommended for employers with 50+ employees. Keep copies of all S01 and S02 forms for at least 7 years.

Common SO1 Mistakes

  • Omitting casual and seasonal workers — all workers paid during the year must be included
  • Forgetting service charge income — tip and service charge distributions are taxable emoluments
  • Mismatched monthly remittances — the #1 cause of TAJ assessments
  • Incorrect TRNs — rejected forms count as a late filing on resubmission
  • Missing non-cash benefits — company cars, housing allowances, and subsidised meals must be declared
  • Confusing SO1 with the NHT annual return — both are due March 31 but filed with different authorities

Jamaica Statutory Rates for 2025/2026

  • PAYE: 25% on income above JMD $1,500,096/year; 30% above JMD $6,000,000
  • NIS: 3% employee + 3% employer (ceiling: JMD $5,000,000)
  • NHT: 2% employee + 3% employer (no ceiling)
  • Education Tax: 2.25% employee + 3.5% employer
  • HEART/NSTA: 3% employer only (10+ employees)

How Payroll Software Automates SO1 Preparation

Payroll software built for Jamaica — like Payroll Jamaica — maintains all 12 months of data and reconciles monthly remittances automatically. At year-end, you export a complete SO1/SO2 package: individual employee returns, the employer aggregate, and a reconciliation report — all pre-populated, formatted for TAJ, and ready to submit. What takes a manual payroll team several days takes under 30 minutes.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need to file if I only have one employee?

Yes. The SO1 requirement applies to all registered employers regardless of headcount, including employers with a single part-time worker.

What if I cannot reconcile before March 31?

File with your best available figures and note the discrepancy. It is better to file on time with a minor error than to miss the deadline. Contact TAJ to arrange a correction.

Can I amend an SO1 after filing?

Yes. Submit an amended return as soon as you discover an error. Prompt correction minimises penalty exposure.
